# 100 MW Solar PV Farm Comparison: Abilene (ERCOT West Hub) vs Houston (ERCOT Houston Hub) — 2023

## Methodology

- **Solar Profiles**: Generated hourly generation (kWh) via Renewables.ninja for a 100 MW (100,000 kW) system with **2-axis (double-axis) tracking**, **10% system loss**, at coordinates for Abilene (32.45°N, −99.75°W) and Houston (29.76°N, −95.37°W) for the full year 2023.
- **LMP Prices**: Real-time (15-minute) locational marginal prices from the ERCOT database for the **West Hub** (Abilene scenario) and **Houston Hub** (Houston scenario), averaged to hourly to match the solar generation profile.
- **Revenue**: Hourly generation (MWh) × hourly LMP ($/MWh), summed annually. No curtailments assumed.

---

## Generation Results

| Metric | Abilene (2-Axis, 10% loss) | Houston (2-Axis, 10% loss) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Generation** | **219,719.71 MWh** | **193,192.80 MWh** | **+26,526.92 MWh (13.7%)** |
| **Capacity Factor** | **25.08%** | **22.05%** | +3.03 pp |
| **Daylight Generating Hours** | 4,467 hrs | 4,409 hrs | +58 hrs |

Abilene (West Texas) enjoys significantly higher solar irradiance and longer daylight hours, producing **13.7% more energy** than the Houston site. This is consistent with West Texas being one of the best solar resources in the U.S.

---

## Price Environment

| Price Metric | ERCOT West Hub | ERCOT Houston Hub |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Avg LMP** | $49.45/MWh | $49.58/MWh |
| **Max Hourly LMP** | $5,257.57/MWh | $5,117.88/MWh |
| **Min Hourly LMP** | −$31.14/MWh | −$83.65/MWh |
| **Avg Price (Solar-hours only)** | $75.77/MWh | $78.05/MWh |
| **Generation-Weighted Avg Price** | **$94.24/MWh** | **$101.40/MWh** |
| **Negative price hours** | 727 hrs (8.3%) | 117 hrs (1.3%) |

**Key insight**: While the simple annual average LMPs are nearly identical, the **generation-weighted average price** is notably higher at Houston Hub ($101.40 vs $94.24). This is because the West Hub experiences far more frequent negative pricing (8.3% of hours vs 1.3%), and those negative-price events often coincide with solar production hours — a well-known phenomenon where abundant West Texas solar depresses mid-day prices. Houston solar producers captured **$7.17/MWh more** on average per unit of generation.

---

## Revenue Results

| Revenue Metric | Abilene @ West Hub | Houston @ Houston Hub | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Annual Revenue** | **$20,705,581** | **$19,590,467** | **+$1,115,114 (5.7%)** |
| **Revenue per MWh** | $94.24/MWh | $101.40/MWh | −$7.17/MWh |
| **Revenue per MW-yr** | $207,056/MW | $195,905/MW | +$11,151/MW |

---

## Monthly Breakdown

## Seasonal Summary

| Season | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue |

| **Winter** (Dec-Feb) | 48,367 | 43,341 | **$1,137,286** | **$1,019,687** |

| Hours with negative prices | 727 (8.3% of year) | 117 (1.3% of year) |
| Revenue lost to negatives | **−$70,318** | **−$2,288** |

The West Hub's significantly higher negative-price exposure is a material risk factor for solar projects in West Texas.

---

## Conclusion

| Winner by factor | Result |
|---|---|
| **Solar Resource** | **Abilene** — generates 13.7% more energy (25.1% vs 22.1% CF) |
| **Realized Price** | **Houston** — earns $101.40/MWh vs $94.24/MWh (7.6% premium) |
| **Total Gross Revenue** | **Abilene** — **$20.7M vs $19.6M (+5.7% or +$1.1M/yr)** |

**The Abilene solar farm paired with ERCOT West Hub pricing generates ~5.7% more annual revenue ($1.1M) than the Houston farm with Houston Hub pricing.** The superior solar resource in West Texas (higher irradiance, clearer skies) outweighs the lower per-MWh realized prices. However, if one were to **locate the Abilene solar farm but sell into the Houston Hub** (via congestion-hedged financial transactions), the economics would improve further — combining Abilene's higher generation with Houston's higher realized prices.

houston_lmp['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(houston_lmp['time'])

# Or more simply: downsample 15-min LMP to hourly by averaging

# Set index for resampling

houston_lmp_hourly = houston_lmp.set_index('datetime').resample('1H')['price'].mean().reset_index()

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import json

# Load data
solar_abilene = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260611_200650.csv')
solar_houston = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260611_200656.csv')
west_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/west_hub_2023.csv')
houston_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/houston_hub_2023.csv')

def extract_electricity(val):
    if isinstance(val, str):
        d = json.loads(val.replace("'", '"'))
        return d['electricity']
    elif isinstance(val, dict):
        return val['electricity']
    return 0

solar_abilene['gen_kwh'] = solar_abilene['value'].apply(extract_electricity)
solar_houston['gen_kwh'] = solar_houston['value'].apply(extract_electricity)
solar_abilene['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_abilene['timestamp'], unit='ms')
solar_houston['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_houston['timestamp'], unit='ms')

west_lmp['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(west_lmp['time'])
houston_lmp['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(houston_lmp['time'])

west_lmp_hourly = west_lmp.set_index('datetime').resample('1h')['price'].mean().reset_index()
houston_lmp_hourly = houston_lmp.set_index('datetime').resample('1h')['price'].mean().reset_index()

solar_abilene = solar_abilene[solar_abilene['datetime_utc'].dt.year == 2023].copy()
solar_houston = solar_houston[solar_houston['datetime_utc'].dt.year == 2023].copy()

solar_abilene['hour'] = solar_abilene['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('1h')
solar_houston['hour'] = solar_houston['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('1h')
west_lmp_hourly['hour'] = west_lmp_hourly['datetime']
houston_lmp_hourly['hour'] = houston_lmp_hourly['datetime']

abilene_west = pd.merge(solar_abilene, west_lmp_hourly, on='hour', how='inner')
houston_hub = pd.merge(solar_houston, houston_lmp_hourly, on='hour', how='inner')

# Only generating hours for weighted average
gen_hours_w = abilene_west[abilene_west['gen_kwh'] > 0]
gen_hours_h = houston_hub[houston_hub['gen_kwh'] > 0]

w_avg_west = np.average(gen_hours_w['price'], weights=gen_hours_w['gen_kwh'])
w_avg_houston = np.average(gen_hours_h['price'], weights=gen_hours_h['gen_kwh'])

print(f"Solar-weighted Avg Price at West Hub (Abilene): ${w_avg_west:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Solar-weighted Avg Price at Houston Hub: ${w_avg_houston:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Difference: ${w_avg_houston - w_avg_west:.2f}/MWh in favor of Houston")

# Also compute revenue/MWh correctly
abilene_west['hourly_revenue'] = abilene_west['gen_kwh'] * abilene_west['price'] / 1000
houston_hub['hourly_revenue'] = houston_hub['gen_kwh'] * houston_hub['price'] / 1000

abilene_total_rev = abilene_west['hourly_revenue'].sum()
houston_total_rev = houston_hub['hourly_revenue'].sum()
abilene_gen = abilene_west['gen_kwh'].sum() / 1000
houston_gen = houston_hub['gen_kwh'].sum() / 1000

print(f"\nAbilene: ${abilene_total_rev:,.2f} / {abilene_gen:,.1f} MWh = ${abilene_total_rev/abilene_gen:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Houston: ${houston_total_rev:,.2f} / {houston_gen:,.1f} MWh = ${houston_total_rev/houston_gen:.2f}/MWh")

# 100 MW Solar PV Farm Comparison: Abilene (ERCOT West Hub) vs Houston (ERCOT Houston Hub) — 2023

## Methodology

- **Solar Profiles**: Generated hourly generation (kWh) via Renewables.ninja for a 100 MW (100,000 kW) system with **2-axis (double-axis) tracking**, **10% system loss**, at coordinates for Abilene (32.45°N, −99.75°W) and Houston (29.76°N, −95.37°W) for the full year 2023.
- **LMP Prices**: Real-time (15-minute) locational marginal prices from the ERCOT database for the **West Hub** (Abilene scenario) and **Houston Hub** (Houston scenario), averaged to hourly to match the solar generation profile.
- **Revenue**: Hourly generation (MWh) × hourly LMP ($/MWh), summed annually. No curtailments assumed.

---

## Generation Results

| Metric | Abilene (2-Axis, 10% loss) | Houston (2-Axis, 10% loss) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Generation** | **219,719.71 MWh** | **193,192.80 MWh** | **+26,526.92 MWh (13.7%)** |
| **Capacity Factor** | **25.08%** | **22.05%** | +3.03 pp |
| **Daylight Generating Hours** | 4,467 hrs | 4,409 hrs | +58 hrs |

Abilene (West Texas) enjoys significantly higher solar irradiance and longer daylight hours, producing **13.7% more energy** than the Houston site. This is consistent with West Texas being one of the best solar resources in the U.S.

---

## Price Environment

| Price Metric | ERCOT West Hub | ERCOT Houston Hub |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Avg LMP** | $49.45/MWh | $49.58/MWh |
| **Max Hourly LMP** | $5,257.57/MWh | $5,117.88/MWh |
| **Min Hourly LMP** | −$31.14/MWh | −$83.65/MWh |
| **Avg Price (Solar-hours only)** | $75.77/MWh | $78.05/MWh |
| **Generation-Weighted Avg Price** | **$94.24/MWh** | **$101.40/MWh** |
| **Negative price hours** | 727 hrs (8.3%) | 117 hrs (1.3%) |

**Key insight**: While the simple annual average LMPs are nearly identical, the **generation-weighted average price** is notably higher at Houston Hub ($101.40 vs $94.24). This is because the West Hub experiences far more frequent negative pricing (8.3% of hours vs 1.3%), and those negative-price events often coincide with solar production hours — a well-known phenomenon where abundant West Texas solar depresses mid-day prices. Houston solar producers captured **$7.17/MWh more** on average per unit of generation.

---

## Revenue Results

| Revenue Metric | Abilene @ West Hub | Houston @ Houston Hub | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Annual Revenue** | **$20,705,581** | **$19,590,467** | **+$1,115,114 (5.7%)** |
| **Revenue per MWh** | $94.24/MWh | $101.40/MWh | −$7.17/MWh |
| **Revenue per MW-yr** | $207,056/MW | $195,905/MW | +$11,151/MW |

---

## Monthly Breakdown

| Month | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | West Avg $/MWh | Houston Avg $/MWh |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 16,385 | 14,253 | $361,936 | $394,971 | $20.56 | $22.87 |
| Feb | 14,809 | 14,071 | $245,953 | $268,839 | $17.70 | $18.98 |
| Mar | 19,001 | 16,135 | $632,304 | $596,845 | $27.24 | $25.91 |
| Apr | 20,842 | 15,870 | $461,433 | $432,760 | $18.31 | $21.11 |
| May | 16,213 | 15,231 | $720,025 | $654,447 | $30.01 | $30.84 |
| **Jun** | **20,017** | **17,902** | **$2,421,042** | **$2,131,853** | **$61.59** | **$62.82** |
| **Jul** | **21,922** | **19,108** | **$1,521,524** | **$1,471,158** | **$42.80** | **$45.62** |
| **Aug** | **21,661** | **18,642** | **$8,870,617** | **$8,748,468** | **$196.37** | **$199.23** |
| Sep | 18,317 | 16,988 | $3,576,581 | $3,352,493 | $89.26 | $86.74 |
| Oct | 16,811 | 16,435 | $707,795 | $688,342 | $29.66 | $28.44 |
| Nov | 16,568 | 13,541 | $656,974 | $494,414 | $31.31 | $29.39 |
| Dec | 17,173 | 15,017 | $529,398 | $355,877 | $25.59 | $20.11 |

**August is the standout month** — extremely high prices (heat-wave-driven scarcity pricing in ERCOT) drove $8.9M and $8.7M in single-month revenues for Abilene and Houston respectively, accounting for ~43% and ~45% of each farm's annual revenue.

---

## Seasonal Summary

| Season | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Winter** (Dec-Feb) | 48,367 | 43,341 | **$1,137,286** | **$1,019,687** |
| **Spring** (Mar-May) | 56,056 | 47,236 | **$1,813,762** | **$1,684,052** |
| **Summer** (Jun-Aug) | 63,600 | 55,652 | **$12,813,183** | **$12,351,479** |
| **Fall** (Sep-Nov) | 51,696 | 46,964 | **$4,941,350** | **$4,535,249** |

---

## Negative Price Exposure

| Metric | Abilene (West Hub) | Houston (Houston Hub) |
|---|---|---|
| Hours with negative prices | 727 (8.3% of year) | 117 (1.3% of year) |
| Revenue lost to negatives | **−$70,318** | **−$2,288** |

The West Hub's significantly higher negative-price exposure is a material risk factor for solar projects in West Texas.

---

## Conclusion

| Winner by factor | Result |
|---|---|
| **Solar Resource** | **Abilene** — generates 13.7% more energy (25.1% vs 22.1% CF) |
| **Realized Price** | **Houston** — earns $101.40/MWh vs $94.24/MWh (7.6% premium) |
| **Total Gross Revenue** | **Abilene** — **$20.7M vs $19.6M (+5.7% or +$1.1M/yr)** |

**The Abilene solar farm paired with ERCOT West Hub pricing generates ~5.7% more annual revenue ($1.1M) than the Houston farm with Houston Hub pricing.** The superior solar resource in West Texas (higher irradiance, clearer skies) outweighs the lower per-MWh realized prices. However, if one were to **locate the Abilene solar farm but sell into the Houston Hub** (via congestion-hedged financial transactions), the economics would improve further — combining Abilene's higher generation with Houston's higher realized prices.
